title: DMA: Moving Data Without the CPU

lessons.title: What DMA Is and Why It Exists
lessons.content:
A **DMA (Direct Memory Access) controller** is a small bus master that moves data between addresses without the CPU executing a load/store for each word. The motivation is throughput and determinism. Consider servicing a UART at 1 Mbaud: an RX interrupt per byte costs you exception entry/exit, register stacking, and the handler body — easily hundreds of cycles per byte, all stolen from your real work, with jitter that grows under load. DMA collapses that to *one* setup and *one* completion event for the whole block.

For a radar/DSP front-end the payoff is sharper: you want an evenly-sampled buffer with the CPU not babysitting each conversion, because per-sample CPU involvement is exactly what introduces sample-to-sample timing jitter. DMA lets a timer trigger the ADC and the ADC trigger the DMA, so a clean buffer materializes in RAM while the core sleeps or does other work.

Mechanically, the DMA controller sits on the **bus matrix** (Module 3) as an alternate master alongside the CPU. When a transfer is armed, the controller arbitrates for the bus, performs the read and write itself, decrements a counter, and signals completion via an interrupt. The CPU is involved only at the edges: *configure*, then *handle completion*.

The mental reframe for a software developer: DMA is a second agent writing your RAM concurrently with the CPU. Everything subtle about DMA — `volatile`, alignment, cache coherency, ordering — flows from that one fact. It is the practical reason the bus-matrix and memory-hazard material earlier in this module mattered.

items.front: In one sentence, what does a DMA controller do?
items.back: It is a bus master that transfers data between addresses (memory or peripheral) on its own, so the CPU does not execute a load/store per word — the core is involved only at configure time and at completion.

items.front: Why does DMA improve determinism, not just CPU load?
items.back: Per-byte or per-sample CPU servicing adds variable interrupt latency and jitter. DMA produces one completion event per block, so acquisition timing is set by the trigger (e.g. a timer) rather than by how busy the CPU is.
items.hint: Think jitter on an evenly-sampled buffer.

items.front: Where does the DMA controller sit in the system, architecturally?
items.back: On the bus matrix (AHB) as an additional bus master alongside the CPU. It arbitrates for the bus, does the read and write itself, and competes with the core for bandwidth.
items.hint: It is not part of the CPU pipeline.

items.front: Name the two points at which the CPU is involved in a DMA transfer.
items.back: Configuration (set source/destination, count, modes, then enable) and completion handling (the transfer-complete, and optionally half-transfer, interrupt). In between, the controller runs autonomously.

items.front: Why is DMA the enabler for an ADC-to-FFT pipeline?
items.back: It captures a contiguous, evenly-sampled block into RAM without dropping samples or adding per-sample jitter, so a DSP task can run a windowed FFT on a clean buffer in real time. Without it, high sample rates overrun the CPU.

items.prompt: At 1 Mbaud, why is per-byte UART RX interrupt servicing problematic compared to DMA?
choices: DMA is slower per byte but uses less RAM
Each byte costs full exception entry/exit overhead and adds jitter, stealing cycles from real work
Interrupts cannot keep up with any UART above 9600 baud
Per-byte interrupts corrupt the data unless parity is enabled
items.explanation: An interrupt per byte pays exception entry/exit, register stacking, and handler cost hundreds of cycles each, with load-dependent jitter. DMA reduces this to one setup and one completion event per block.

items.prompt: The DMA controller competes with the CPU for what shared resource?
choices: The register file (R0-R12)
The NVIC priority levels
The bus matrix / memory bus bandwidth
The floating-point unit
items.explanation: DMA is a separate bus master. It and the CPU arbitrate for access to the AHB bus matrix and the memory it targets; heavy DMA traffic can stall CPU accesses, and vice versa.

items.prompt: Which statement best captures the core reframe DMA demands of a software developer?
choices: DMA is just a faster memcpy that always blocks until done
DMA is a second concurrent agent writing your RAM, so coherency and ordering rules apply
DMA replaces the need for interrupts entirely
DMA only works for memory-to-memory copies
items.explanation: DMA writes memory concurrently with the CPU. Every subtle DMA hazard (volatile, alignment, cache coherency, ordering) follows from treating it as a concurrent writer, not a synchronous copy.

items.prompt: A timer triggers the ADC and the ADC triggers DMA. What sets the sample timing?
choices: The DMA arbitration priority
The CPU interrupt latency
The timer's hardware trigger rate, independent of CPU load
The UART baud rate
items.explanation: With hardware triggering, the timer defines the exact, jitter-free sample instant. The CPU's busyness no longer affects when samples are taken — only when buffers are processed.

lessons.title: The Controller Model: Channels, Directions, and Triggers
lessons.content:
A DMA transfer is described by a handful of fields you program into the controller. The **channel** (STM32 F4/F7 calls the data path a *stream* with a selectable *channel/request*; many parts just say *channel*) is the independent transfer engine. Each channel carries a **source address**, a **destination address**, a **transfer count** (number of items), an **item width** (byte/half-word/word), and **increment modes** that say whether the source and/or destination pointer advances after each item. Memory pointers usually increment; a peripheral data register usually does not. When several channels want the bus at once, **arbitration/priority** decides order.

Three direction archetypes cover almost everything. **Memory-to-memory** is a hardware `memcpy` (both pointers increment). **Peripheral-to-memory** drains a peripheral (e.g. ADC data register, fixed) into a growing buffer (incrementing). **Memory-to-peripheral** does the reverse — e.g. streaming a waveform table to a DAC or bytes to a UART TX register.

Crucially, most useful DMA is **peripheral-triggered**, not free-running. The peripheral raises a *DMA request* on an event — ADC conversion complete, UART RX-not-empty, timer update — and the controller performs exactly one item transfer per request. This is what couples acquisition rate to hardware timing rather than to a software loop. Memory-to-memory, by contrast, runs as fast as the bus allows.

A recurring pitfall: width and increment must match the data. Reading a 12-bit ADC into a `uint8_t` buffer, or forgetting to disable destination increment when targeting a single register, silently produces garbage with no fault. Get item width, increment flags, and count right before anything else.

items.front: List the core fields that define a single DMA transfer.
items.back: Source address, destination address, transfer count (items), item width (byte/half-word/word), source and destination increment modes, and a priority used for arbitration. Plus the trigger source for peripheral-driven transfers.
items.hint: From, to, how many, how wide, who moves the pointer.

items.front: What is a DMA channel (or 'stream') and why have several?
items.back: An independent transfer engine with its own configuration. Multiple channels let different peripherals run concurrent transfers; when more than one is ready, arbitration/priority decides which gets the bus next.

items.front: For peripheral-to-memory ADC capture, which pointers increment?
items.back: The memory (destination) pointer increments so successive samples land in successive buffer slots. The peripheral (source) address is fixed on the ADC data register, so its increment is OFF.
items.hint: The register stays put; the buffer fills.

items.back: The peripheral asserts a DMA request on an event (conversion complete, RX byte ready, timer update); the controller moves exactly one item per request. Transfer pacing is set by the peripheral, not a CPU loop.

items.front: Name the three DMA direction archetypes with an example each.
items.back: Memory-to-memory (hardware memcpy, both increment); peripheral-to-memory (ADC data reg to RAM buffer); memory-to-peripheral (waveform table to DAC, or TX buffer to UART data register).

items.prompt: You DMA a 12-bit ADC result into a buffer but set the item width to byte (8-bit). What happens?
choices: A bus fault is raised immediately
The DMA truncates correctly to 8 bits with a warning flag
Data is silently misaligned/garbled with no error reported
The transfer count automatically doubles to compensate
items.explanation: DMA width/increment mismatches do not fault. A 12-bit result needs half-word width; using byte width misreads the data register and corrupts the buffer silently. Width and increment must match the data.

items.prompt: When targeting a single peripheral register (e.g. DAC data register) from memory, the destination increment should be:
choices: Enabled, so the pointer walks the peripheral block
Disabled, so every item writes the same fixed register address
Set to half the item width
Irrelevant for memory-to-peripheral transfers
items.explanation: The destination is one fixed register, so its increment must be OFF while the memory source increments through the waveform/buffer. Enabling it would write past the register into neighboring peripheral addresses.

items.prompt: Two DMA channels are simultaneously ready to move data. What resolves the contention?
choices: The NVIC interrupt priority of each peripheral
The DMA channel arbitration/priority setting
Whichever channel was configured first always wins
The CPU must manually alternate them in software
items.explanation: Each channel has a programmable priority; the DMA controller's arbiter uses it (with a fixed tie-break, often lowest channel number) to decide bus access order. This is distinct from NVIC interrupt priority.

items.prompt: Which transfer typically runs 'as fast as the bus allows' rather than being event-paced?
choices: Peripheral-to-memory ADC capture
Memory-to-peripheral UART TX
Memory-to-memory copy
Timer-triggered DAC playback
items.explanation: Memory-to-memory has no external request source, so the controller bursts items back-to-back limited only by bus availability. The other three are gated by a peripheral or timer event.

lessons.title: Streaming, Ping-Pong, and the Cache/Coherency Traps
lessons.content:
Continuous acquisition needs a buffer scheme that never stalls. **Circular mode** wraps the DMA pointer back to the buffer start automatically at the end of the count, so the stream runs forever. The standard processing pattern is **double-buffering (ping-pong)**: treat the buffer as two halves and use the controller's **half-transfer (HT)** and **transfer-complete (TC)** interrupts. On HT, the first half is full and stable while DMA fills the second; on TC, the second is full while DMA wraps into the first. The CPU always processes the half the DMA is *not* writing — no torn reads, no lost samples.

The real rust-knocker is **coherency**. DMA writes RAM behind the CPU's back, so two things can lie to you. First, the compiler: a DMA buffer (and any flag the ISR sets) must be treated as **`volatile`** / accessed through a barrier, or the compiler may cache a stale value in a register and never re-read the RAM the DMA updated. `volatile` alone gives no atomicity and no ordering versus non-volatile accesses — add `__DMB()` where ordering matters.

Second, and worse, **the data cache on Cortex-M7**. The DMA writes SRAM, but the CPU may read a *stale cache line* and never see the new data; symmetrically, on a TX buffer the CPU's writes may sit in the cache and never reach SRAM before DMA reads it. The fixes: before the CPU reads a DMA-filled buffer, **invalidate** that region; before DMA reads a CPU-filled buffer, **clean** (flush) it; or place the buffer in an **MPU non-cacheable region**. Both cache ops operate on whole cache lines, so the buffer must be **cache-line aligned** (typically 32 bytes) and sized in line multiples — otherwise an invalidate can clobber an adjacent variable sharing the line. Finally, watch **bus bandwidth**: a fast DMA stream and the CPU share the matrix, so a heavy transfer can stall the core and inflate worst-case timing.

items.front: What does DMA circular mode do?
items.back: At the end of the transfer count, the controller automatically reloads and wraps the pointer to the buffer start, so the stream runs continuously without CPU re-arming. It is the basis for never-stopping acquisition.

items.front: Explain the ping-pong (double-buffer) pattern using HT and TC interrupts.
items.back: Split the circular buffer in two halves. The half-transfer (HT) IRQ fires when the first half is full (DMA now filling the second); the transfer-complete (TC) IRQ fires when the second is full (DMA wrapping to the first). The CPU processes the half DMA is not writing.
items.hint: Process one half while the other fills.

items.front: Why must a DMA buffer (and its done-flag) be volatile?
items.back: DMA updates the RAM independently of the CPU. Without volatile/barriers the compiler may keep a stale copy in a register and never re-read the memory the DMA changed, so the CPU misses the new data or the completion flag.
items.hint: Stop the compiler from caching in a register.

items.front: Does volatile solve the Cortex-M7 data-cache coherency problem?
items.back: No. Volatile stops the *compiler* caching in registers, but the hardware data cache still holds stale lines. You additionally need cache maintenance (invalidate before read, clean before DMA reads your writes) or a non-cacheable MPU region.
items.hint: Two different layers: compiler vs hardware cache.

items.front: On Cortex-M7, which cache op before reading a DMA-filled RX buffer, and which before a DMA reads your TX buffer?
items.back: Before the CPU reads a DMA-filled buffer: INVALIDATE that region (discard stale cache lines, force re-read from SRAM). Before DMA reads a CPU-written buffer: CLEAN/flush it (push the CPU's cached writes out to SRAM).
items.hint: Invalidate for RX, clean for TX.

items.front: Why must DMA buffers be cache-line aligned and line-sized on M7?
items.back: Cache maintenance operates on whole lines (typically 32 bytes). If a buffer shares a line with another variable, invalidating the buffer can discard that neighbor's freshly written value (or vice versa), corrupting it. Align to and pad to the line size.
items.hint: Invalidate hits the whole line, including the neighbor.

items.prompt: On a Cortex-M7, a DMA fills an RX buffer in SRAM but the CPU keeps reading the old contents. The most likely cause is:
choices: The buffer was not declared const
The CPU read a stale data-cache line; the region was not invalidated before reading
The DMA priority was set too low
Circular mode was disabled
items.explanation: DMA writes SRAM directly, but the CPU's D-cache may still hold the old line. You must invalidate the buffer's cache lines (or mark it non-cacheable) before reading so the CPU fetches the DMA-updated data from SRAM.

items.prompt: You invalidate a 12-byte DMA buffer that happens to share a 32-byte cache line with an unrelated counter the CPU just wrote. What is the risk?
choices: Nothing; invalidate only affects the named buffer
The counter's freshly written value is discarded because invalidate acts on the whole line
The DMA transfer count is reset to zero
The MPU raises a fault on the partial line
items.explanation: Cache maintenance works per line. Invalidating discards the entire line including the neighboring counter's not-yet-flushed value, corrupting it. Align and pad DMA buffers to whole cache lines so nothing else shares them.

items.prompt: In ADC ping-pong, the half-transfer (HT) interrupt signals that:
choices: The DMA has stopped and must be re-armed
The first half of the buffer is full and stable to process while DMA fills the second half
Exactly one sample has been transferred
A bus error occurred mid-transfer
items.explanation: HT fires at the midpoint: the first half is complete and not being touched, so the CPU can process it while DMA continues into the second half. TC then signals the second half is ready.

items.prompt: An alternative to per-transfer cache maintenance for DMA buffers on Cortex-M7 is to:
choices: Disable all interrupts during the transfer
Place the buffer in an MPU-configured non-cacheable memory region
Use byte-width transfers only
Increase the DMA channel priority to maximum
items.explanation: Configuring the buffer's region as non-cacheable via the MPU means the CPU always goes to SRAM, so no clean/invalidate is needed. The trade-off is slower CPU access to that region; explicit maintenance keeps the cache benefit.

items.prompt: A high-rate DMA stream coincides with a tight CPU deadline and the worst-case latency grows. The mechanism is:
choices: DMA raises the CPU clock frequency
Bus-matrix contention: DMA and CPU share bandwidth, so heavy DMA stalls CPU accesses
DMA disables the NVIC during transfers
The cache is flushed automatically on every DMA cycle
items.explanation: DMA is a competing bus master. When it saturates the matrix or the targeted memory, the CPU's loads/stores wait, inflating worst-case execution time. Bus bandwidth and arbitration must be budgeted in real-time analysis.
